threats.title: Isolate Liquidation Transfers NFT to Caller Instead of Highest Bidder - Direct Asset Theft
threats.hypothesis_description: In executeIsolateLiquidate, the NFT collateral is transferred to params.msgSender (the caller) rather than loanData.lastBidder (the auction winner). The highest bidder's escrowed funds are used to repay the loan debt, but the NFT is given to whoever calls isolateLiquidate after the auction ends. Any address can call this function with zero cost (when bidAmount >= borrowAmount) and steal the NFT from the legitimate auction winner.
threats.investigation_rationale: The vulnerability is a direct code defect. The auction winner's bid funds are consumed (via erc20TransferOutBidAmountToLiqudity) but the NFT goes to the caller. There is no access control check restricting the caller to be the last bidder. The validateIsolateLiquidateLoan only checks loan status and reserve asset matching.

proof_of_signal.pattern: erc721TransferIsolateSupplyOnLiquidate(nftAssetData, params.msgSender, ...) and erc721TransferOutLiquidity(nftAssetData, params.msgSender, ...) use params.msgSender instead of loanData.lastBidder

preconditions: An isolate loan is in AUCTION status
Auction duration has ended (block.timestamp > bidStartTimestamp + auctionDuration)
Attacker has address different from loanData.lastBidder

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: NFT is transferred to params.msgSender rather than loanData.lastBidder across both supplyAsCollateral branches
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None - any EOA can exploit this
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: From auction end time until someone calls isolateLiquidate

operational_questions: Is there an off-chain component that restricts who can call isolateLiquidate?
Can the highest bidder race to call this first to protect themselves?
validation_requirements: Deploy test: have bidder A win auction, then have attacker B call isolateLiquidate and verify B receives the NFT
Verify validateIsolateLiquidateLoan has no caller==lastBidder check

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Monitor for isolate loans entering AUCTION status with valuable NFTs
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Wait for auction end timestamp to pass
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Call isolateLiquidate with supplyAsCollateral=false to receive the NFT directly
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: If bidAmount < borrowAmount, pay the small extra difference; if bidAmount >= borrowAmount, pay nothing
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None - single transaction exploit by any caller

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Complete destruction of auction mechanism trust; all bidders lose funds
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Stolen NFTs cannot be recovered without bidder/victim cooperation
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All isolate lending auctions become unsafe for bidders

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Basic - call one public function after auction ends

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Monitor isolateLiquidate calls where msgSender != loanData.lastBidder
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Repeated calls from same address across multiple auctions

threats.title: Flash Loan ERC20 Missing Reentrancy Guard Enables State Manipulation During Callback
threats.hypothesis_description: flashLoanERC20 intentionally omits the nonReentrant modifier. During the callback to the flash loan receiver, the reentrancy lock is not set, and availableLiquidity is not decremented for the flash-loaned tokens. This allows the callback to re-enter any protocol function (deposit, borrow, liquidate, repay) while seeing inflated availableLiquidity. When there are active isolate auctions with bid amounts in the same asset, the actual token balance exceeds (availableLiquidity - flashLoanAmount) by totalBidAmout, allowing borrows that drain bid escrow tokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: The comment in FlashLoan.sol explicitly states 'Don't use nonReentrant modifier here'. The shared reentrancy lock in Base storage is NOT engaged, verified by examining flashLoanERC20 modifier list. Available liquidity is tracked by assetData.availableLiquidity which is never decremented by erc20TransferOutOnFlashLoan. Operations during callback see the pre-flash-loan availableLiquidity value.

proof_of_signal.pattern: flashLoanERC20 has only whenNotPaused modifier, no nonReentrant; comment explicitly states to not use nonReentrant

preconditions: Flash loan enabled on the target ERC20 asset
Active isolate auctions with non-zero totalBidAmout in the same asset (for fund drain vector)
Attacker deploys a malicious IFlashLoanReceiver contract

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: erc20TransferOutOnFlashLoan bypasses availableLiquidity tracking; reentrancy lock unset during callback
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Entire duration of flash loan callback

operational_questions: What is the maximum totalBidAmout typically present for high-liquidity assets?
Can interest rate manipulation via inflated utilization create profitable arbitrage?
Does the borrow path's erc20TransferOutLiquidity balance check prevent draining bid escrow?
validation_requirements: Test: flash loan full available liquidity, then borrow during callback when bid amounts exist
Verify erc20TransferOutLiquidity uses actual balance check that could be satisfied by bid escrow tokens
Test interest rate manipulation: flash loan → deposit → borrow during callback

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Deploy malicious flash loan receiver contract
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Flash loan maximum available liquidity of target asset
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: During callback: deposit collateral in a different asset, then borrow the target asset against inflated availableLiquidity
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Borrowed tokens come from bid escrow; repay flash loan from the borrowed tokens plus existing funds

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Bid escrow tokens drained; auction bidders cannot recover their bids; interest rate manipulation affects all borrowers
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires protocol pause and manual accounting correction
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All borrowing, lending, and auction operations on the affected asset

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Advanced - custom flash loan receiver contract
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: When sufficient bid amounts are escrowed in the target asset

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Flash loan callbacks that invoke borrow or deposit functions
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: availableLiquidity + totalBidAmout > actual token balance after flash loan operations
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Flash loans immediately followed by borrows from same address

threats.title: Isolate Redeem Decreases Borrow Balance of Caller Instead of Actual Borrower - Accounting Corruption
threats.hypothesis_description: In executeIsolateRedeem, the call to erc20DecreaseIsolateScaledBorrow uses params.msgSender (the caller/redeemer) instead of the actual borrower (tokenData.owner). This means: (1) if an approved operator or third party calls redeem, their own isolate borrow is decreased instead of the borrower's, (2) the loan's scaledAmount is correctly decreased but the borrower's userScaledIsolateBorrow stays inflated, creating permanent phantom debt that can never be fully repaid, and (3) totalScaledIsolateBorrow becomes inconsistent with the sum of user balances.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code explicitly passes params.msgSender to erc20DecreaseIsolateScaledBorrow at IsolateLogic.sol line ~195. The original borrow was increased for params.onBehalf in executeIsolateBorrow. The redeem should decrease the same account's borrow, but instead decreases the caller's. This is a clear code defect.

proof_of_signal.pattern: VaultLogic.erc20DecreaseIsolateScaledBorrow(debtGroupData, params.msgSender, vars.amountScaled) - uses params.msgSender instead of tokenData.owner or loanData original borrower

preconditions: An isolate loan is in AUCTION status
Auction has not ended (block.timestamp <= auctionEndTimestamp)
Caller has their own isolate borrow in the same group with sufficient scaled balance

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: params.msgSender used as borrow decrease target instead of the NFT owner who originally borrowed
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: During any active isolate auction's redeem window

operational_questions: Can an attacker with isolate borrows in the same group get free debt reduction by redeeming others' loans?
What happens to the original borrower's position after redeem corrupts their accounting?
Can the phantom debt cause permanent lockup of the borrower's NFT?
validation_requirements: Test: User A has isolate borrow, User A's loan enters auction, User B (with isolate borrow in same group) calls redeem
Verify User B's borrow decreased while User A's borrow unchanged
Verify User A can never fully repay because their userScaledIsolateBorrow exceeds actual loan scaledAmount

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker creates isolate borrow positions in the same asset group as the target
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Wait for another user's loan to enter auction
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Call isolateRedeem for the target loan - attacker's own debt gets reduced by redeemThreshold amount
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attacker pays redeem amount + bid fine, but gets equivalent debt reduction on their own account
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires existing isolate borrow position in same group

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Corrupted per-user borrow accounting; phantom debt prevents borrower from ever fully clearing their position; NFTs permanently locked
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires protocol upgrade to fix accounting manually
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All isolate lending redeem operations; affected user positions permanently broken

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Low - single transaction, but requires existing isolate borrow

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Redeem calls where caller != NFT owner/borrower
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Users calling redeem on others' loans repeatedly

threats.title: Native Token Withdraw Uses Wrong Amount for WETH Unwrap - Transaction Revert or Excess WETH Drain
threats.hypothesis_description: In BVault.withdrawERC20 with native token, executeWithdrawERC20 may cap the withdrawal amount to the user's actual balance (when requested amount > balance). However, the original uncapped amount is passed to unwrapNativeTokenInWallet, which attempts to transferFrom(user, pool, originalAmount) of WETH. Since the user received less WETH than originalAmount, and potentially the WETH was sent to receiver (not msgSender), the unwrap fails. This breaks all native ETH partial withdrawal attempts and 'withdraw max' patterns.
threats.investigation_rationale: BVault.withdrawERC20 passes local variable 'amount' to unwrapNativeTokenInWallet. The struct modification inside executeWithdrawERC20 (params.amount = userBalance) changes the memory struct parameter but not the calling function's local 'amount'. Additionally, executeWithdrawERC20 sends WETH to params.receiver, while unwrapNativeTokenInWallet pulls from msgSender - these may be different addresses.

proof_of_signal.pattern: unwrapNativeTokenInWallet(asset, msgSender, amount) uses the original 'amount' parameter, not the potentially-capped amount from executeWithdrawERC20

preconditions: User withdraws native token (NATIVE_TOKEN_ADDRESS) with amount > userBalance, or uses type(uint256).max
wrappedNativeToken is configured in PoolStorage

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Amount capping in executeWithdrawERC20 doesn't propagate back to BVault's unwrap call
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None - affects all native token withdrawals
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any native token withdrawal where amount != exact user balance

operational_questions: Does the WETH implementation allow self-transfer without explicit approval?
When receiver != msgSender, does the entire native withdrawal path fail?
How many users will attempt type(uint256).max withdrawals?
validation_requirements: Test: deposit 5 ETH, withdraw 10 ETH as native, verify revert
Test: deposit 5 ETH, withdraw type(uint256).max as native
Test: withdraw with receiver != msgSender for native path

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: This is primarily a denial-of-service / broken functionality issue
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Users attempting standard 'withdraw all' native ETH operations will have transactions revert
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: If user has pre-existing WETH balance and approval, excess WETH could be pulled from them

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Native ETH withdrawal broken for partial/max withdrawals; users must know exact balance to withdraw
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Users can work around by withdrawing as WETH instead of native ETH
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All native ETH withdrawals where amount != exact balance

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: None - normal user action triggers the bug

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Reverted withdrawERC20 transactions with native token address
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: High failure rate on native ETH withdrawals
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Users switching to WETH withdrawals instead of native

threats.title: Native Token Borrow Flow Broken - WETH Sent to Receiver but Unwrap Pulls from MsgSender
threats.hypothesis_description: In CrossLending.crossBorrowERC20 with native token, executeCrossBorrowERC20 sends the borrowed WETH to params.receiver. Then unwrapNativeTokenInWallet attempts to transferFrom(msgSender, pool, totalBorrowAmount) to pull WETH from msgSender for unwrapping. When receiver != msgSender (or even when they're equal but msgSender hasn't approved the protocol for WETH), this call fails. The native ETH borrowing path is fundamentally broken because WETH is sent outward then an attempt is made to pull it back.
threats.investigation_rationale: The WETH flow requires: (1) borrow sends WETH to receiver, (2) unwrap pulls WETH from msgSender, withdraws to ETH, sends ETH to msgSender. Step 2 requires msgSender to have WETH and have approved the protocol. But the WETH was sent to receiver in step 1. Even with receiver==msgSender, the user needs explicit WETH approval for the pool, which native ETH borrowers wouldn't have.

proof_of_signal.pattern: executeCrossBorrowERC20 sends WETH to receiver param; then unwrapNativeTokenInWallet tries IWETH.transferFrom(msgSender, address(this), totalBorrowAmount)

preconditions: User calls crossBorrowERC20 with NATIVE_TOKEN_ADDRESS as the asset
User hasn't pre-approved WETH spending for the protocol proxy

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: WETH is transferred OUT to receiver, then unwrap tries to pull WETH FROM msgSender

operational_questions: Is there a WETH implementation where transferFrom(user, pool) works without approval when called by pool?
Is receiver always expected to be msgSender for native borrows?
Does the same issue affect isolateBorrow with native tokens?
validation_requirements: Test: borrow with NATIVE_TOKEN_ADDRESS, verify transaction reverts due to WETH allowance
Confirm isolateBorrow has the same issue (IsolateLending.sol follows identical pattern)

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Not an attack vector per se, but a broken feature that blocks native ETH borrowing
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Users attempting native ETH borrows will have all transactions revert

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires module upgrade to fix the unwrap flow
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: crossBorrowERC20 and isolateBorrow with native token

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Reverted borrow transactions with native token address
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Users forced to borrow WETH instead of ETH

threats.title: ERC721 Flash Loan Bypasses lockerAddr Check - Locked NFTs Can Be Temporarily Extracted
threats.hypothesis_description: executeFlashLoanERC721 validates that tokenData.owner == msgSender and checks isolate loan status, but does NOT check tokenData.lockerAddr. This allows NFT owners to flash-loan NFTs that are locked by the isolate lending system (lockerAddr set to protocol address) or by yield managers. During the callback, the NFT is physically outside the pool, breaking the guarantee that locked NFTs remain in the protocol's custody.
threats.investigation_rationale: The flash loan validation in FlashLoanLogic.sol checks owner and loan status but has no lockerAddr check. Meanwhile, withdrawERC721 and setERC721SupplyMode both validate lockerAddr == address(0). The flash loan bypasses this critical safety check. During the callback, yield managers or isolate borrow operations that assume the NFT is in the pool could behave incorrectly.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Checks tokenData.owner == inputParams.msgSender and loanData.loanStatus but NOT tokenData.lockerAddr == address(0)

preconditions: NFT is deposited in isolate mode with an active loan (lockerAddr != address(0))
NFT asset has isFlashLoanEnabled == true
Attacker is the NFT owner (tokenData.owner)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Missing lockerAddr validation in flash loan path vs present in withdraw/mode-change paths
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time a locked NFT's owner calls flash loan

operational_questions: Can yield managers be disrupted during the callback window when the NFT is outside the pool?
Can the flash loan callback interact with the NFT in ways that should be prevented by the lock?
Could airdrop claims or other NFT utility be extracted during the callback?
validation_requirements: Test: deposit NFT in isolate mode, borrow against it, then flash loan the same NFT
Verify the NFT leaves the pool during callback despite lockerAddr being set

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Deposit NFT in isolate mode and borrow against it (lockerAddr gets set)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Flash loan the same NFT out of the pool
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: During callback, perform actions with the NFT (claim airdrops, interact with external contracts, etc.)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Return NFT to pool; lock guarantee was temporarily violated

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Lock guarantee violated; yield managers may see inconsistent state; airdrop/utility extraction
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: NFT returned after flash loan; damage depends on callback actions
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Yield staking, isolate lending lock guarantees

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Any time when NFT has active loan

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: ERC721 flash loans on NFTs with non-zero lockerAddr
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: NFT safeTransferFrom events for locked tokens
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Flash loans on NFTs immediately after borrowing against them

threats.title: Fee Collection Order-of-Operations Bug - Treasury Receives Slightly Inflated Supply Shares
threats.hypothesis_description: In executeCollectFeeToTreasury, the accruedFee is read and zeroed BEFORE calling updateInterestIndexs. Then updateInterestIndexs updates the supply index and accrues new fees. The old accruedFee is then multiplied by the NEW (higher) normalizedIncome to determine amountToCollect. This means the treasury receives supply shares calculated at the updated index for fees that were accrued at the old index, resulting in slightly more shares than warranted. The difference is extracted from existing suppliers.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code at PoolLogic.sol reads accruedFee, zeros it, then calls updateInterestIndexs which modifies supplyIndex. The subsequent rayMul with getNormalizedSupplyIncome uses the new index. The fee was accumulated as accruedFee += amount.rayDiv(oldSupplyIndex), but is collected as accruedFee.rayMul(newSupplyIndex), so if supplyIndex increased between accumulation and collection, the treasury gets a windfall.

proof_of_signal.pattern: accruedFee read and zeroed at line ~50, updateInterestIndexs called at line ~55, normalizedIncome computed at line ~56 using updated index

preconditions: Fee factor > 0 for the asset
Time has passed since last index update (supply rate > 0)
Pool admin calls collectFeeToTreasury

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: accruedFee zeroed before index update, then old fee multiplied by new index
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Triggered by pool admin but the bug is in the code logic
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Every fee collection call when time has elapsed since last update

operational_questions: What is the magnitude of the excess shares per collection?
Over many collections, does the cumulative excess amount become significant?
Could admin strategically time collections to maximize the discrepancy?
validation_requirements: Compare fee collected with correct ordering vs current ordering
Quantify the excess per collection as a function of time elapsed and supply rate

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: This is a systemic accounting error, not a direct attack vector
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Admin could maximize the effect by calling collectFeeToTreasury after long periods without any pool activity
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: The longer since last index update, the larger the index jump and the larger the treasury windfall
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Pool admin access required to trigger collection

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Slow dilution of supplier positions; treasury receives more shares than earned
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires code fix; past excess shares cannot be easily reversed
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Fee collection mechanism; affects all suppliers' share value

execution_requirements_analysis.governance_tokens_needed: None for the bug; pool admin role for triggering
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: More impactful after longer periods without interaction

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Compare collected fee amounts vs expected based on borrow activity
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Treasury share balance growing faster than fee accrual rate

threats.title: Flash Loan ERC20 Enables Interest Rate Manipulation via Inflated Available Liquidity During Callback
threats.hypothesis_description: During a flashLoanERC20 callback, any protocol operation that calls updateInterestRates computes utilization using assetData.availableLiquidity (which is NOT decremented by the flash loan). This inflates the denominator in the utilization calculation (availableLiquidity + liquidityAdded - liquidityTaken + totalDebt), producing artificially low utilization rates. Borrowers can exploit this to lock in lower interest rates by borrowing during the callback. The lower-than-market rates benefit borrowers at the expense of lenders who receive less yield.
threats.investigation_rationale: updateInterestRates uses assetData.availableLiquidity to compute utilization. erc20TransferOutOnFlashLoan does not touch availableLiquidity. During callback, if any operation triggers updateInterestRates (deposit, borrow, repay, liquidate all call it), the rates are computed with inflated liquidity. The stored borrowRate and supplyRate are updated to these manipulated values.

proof_of_signal.pattern: availableLiquidityPlusDebt = assetData.availableLiquidity + liquidityAdded - liquidityTaken + totalAssetDebt; utilization calculated from this inflated value

preconditions: Flash loan enabled on the target asset
Attacker can deploy a custom flash loan receiver that re-enters the protocol
Meaningful outstanding debt exists (so utilization/rate changes have measurable effect)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: availableLiquidity unchanged during flash loan; rate calculations use this inflated value

operational_questions: How much can utilization be suppressed per flash loan?
Do the manipulated rates persist after the flash loan completes?
What is the economic gain from borrowing at artificially low rates?
validation_requirements: Compare interest rates computed during flash loan callback vs normal conditions
Verify rates stored on-chain reflect the manipulated values
Compute attacker profit from rate differential over reasonable borrow duration

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Flash loan large amount of target asset
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: During callback: trigger a small repay or deposit to force updateInterestRates with inflated liquidity
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Immediately after rate update: borrow at the now-lower stored rate
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Repay flash loan; enjoy below-market borrowing rate until next rate update

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Interest rates manipulated below fair market value; lenders receive less yield
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Rates self-correct on next operation, but attacker locks in low rate for their borrow
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All interest rate-sensitive operations on affected asset

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: When there's sufficient liquidity to flash loan

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Flash loan callbacks that include repay/deposit/borrow operations on the same asset
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Sudden rate drops coinciding with flash loan events
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Repeated flash loan + borrow sequences from same address

threats.title: Cross Liquidation ERC721 - Incorrect collateralItemDebtToCover Division Creates Rounding Exploit for Multi-NFT Liquidation
threats.hypothesis_description: In _calculateDebtAmountFromERC721Collateral, the per-item debt coverage is calculated as collateralTotalDebtToCover / userCollateralBalance (integer division). When the result is small or has significant rounding loss, the liquidation price per NFT may be significantly lower than the fair proportion. With multiple NFTs being liquidated, the total debtAmountNeeded (which uses the per-item liquidate price × number of tokens) can underpay the actual proportional debt, allowing the liquidator to acquire NFTs for less than their fair debt coverage.
threats.investigation_rationale: The division collateralTotalDebtToCover / liqVars.userCollateralBalance rounds down in Solidity. For small debt-to-collateral ratios or large userCollateralBalance, this truncation loses significant value. The liquidator pays debtAmountNeeded which is based on the truncated per-item value multiplied by the number of tokens being liquidated.

proof_of_signal.pattern: vars.collateralItemDebtToCover = vars.collateralTotalDebtToCover / liqVars.userCollateralBalance; integer division truncation amplified by multiplication

preconditions: User has multiple ERC721 collateral with cross-margin borrows
Health factor < 1 triggering liquidation eligibility
User's debt-to-collateral ratio creates significant rounding in per-item division

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Integer division of total debt by total NFT count, then multiplication back by liquidated count

operational_questions: What is the maximum rounding loss per NFT in base currency terms?
Can an attacker structure their position to maximize the rounding benefit?
Does the remainDebtToLiquidate correctly handle the discrepancy?
validation_requirements: Calculate concrete rounding loss examples with real NFT collection sizes
Test multi-NFT liquidation with edge case debt amounts

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Deposit many NFTs as cross collateral
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Borrow amount designed to create maximum per-item rounding loss
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Liquidator liquidates subset of NFTs paying less than proportional debt

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Underpayment on liquidations; bad debt accumulation
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Cumulative rounding losses are not recoverable

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Advanced - precise calculation of optimal position
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: When NFT prices drop below liquidation threshold

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: ERC721 liquidations where debtAmountNeeded < expected proportional debt
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Repeated positions with unusual NFT counts and debt amounts

threats.title: Isolate Auction First Bid Minimum Can Create Permanently Unliquidatable Bad Debt
threats.hypothesis_description: The first bid in an isolate auction must be >= borrowAmount AND >= liquidatePrice. If the NFT's market value drops significantly below the accumulated debt, no rational liquidator will bid because they must pay at least the full borrow amount to acquire an NFT worth less. This creates permanently unliquidatable bad debt positions, locking lender funds in the pool indefinitely.
threats.investigation_rationale: In executeIsolateAuction, the first bid requires: (1) borrowAmount > thresholdPrice (health factor < 1), (2) bidAmount >= borrowAmount, (3) bidAmount >= liquidatePrice. When NFT value << borrowAmount, condition (2) requires bidders to overpay. No rational actor will bid 100 ETH of debt for an NFT worth 10 ETH.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(params.amounts[vars.nidx] >= vars.borrowAmount) enforces first bid >= total accumulated debt regardless of NFT value

preconditions: NFT floor price drops significantly (e.g., 80%+ decline)
Accumulated debt with interest exceeds NFT market value
No liquidator willing to take the loss on the bid

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: First bid minimum pegged to borrowAmount not NFT value

operational_questions: What historical NFT price drops have exceeded liquidation bonus thresholds?
How much bad debt could accumulate across all isolate positions?
Is there an alternative liquidation path for underwater positions?
validation_requirements: Model NFT price scenarios where borrowAmount exceeds NFT value by various multiples
Verify no alternative mechanism exists to clear the bad debt

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: This is a systemic risk rather than a direct attack
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: During NFT market downturns, underwater positions cannot be liquidated
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Bad debt accumulates, reducing effective liquidity for all lenders
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: If widespread enough, could trigger protocol insolvency

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Permanent bad debt; lender funds locked; potential cascading insolvency
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires governance intervention or socialized loss mechanism
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All isolate lending positions and lender withdrawals

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Isolate loans where borrowAmount >> NFT floor price with no auction bids
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Growing number of underwater positions with no liquidation activity
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Absence of auction activity for specific asset groups

threats.title: Cross Liquidation ERC721 remainDebtToLiquidate Creates Unbacked Supply Shares When Debt > Actual Repayment
threats.hypothesis_description: In executeCrossLiquidateERC721, when _repayUserERC20Debt cannot fully repay all groups (remainDebtToLiquidate > 0), the remainder is added as borrower's cross supply via erc20IncreaseCrossSupply. However, the liquidator pays actualDebtToLiquidate (the full calculated amount), and the repayment function iterates groups from highest to lowest rate. If rounding causes individual group repayments to consume more than the proportional share, the sum of actual group repayments might be less than actualDebtToLiquidate, leaving a remainder that becomes phantom supply.
threats.investigation_rationale: The _repayUserERC20Debt converts actualDebtToLiquidate to per-group repayments using curDebtRepayAmount = borrowInGroup.rayMul(borrowIndex). The rayMul rounding can cause small discrepancies. When remainDebtToLiquidate > 0, it becomes borrower supply without corresponding real tokens beyond what the liquidator paid. The actual token transfer of actualDebtToLiquidate goes to liquidity, but remainDebtToLiquidate as supply is minted from the same pool.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (vars.remainDebtToLiquidate > 0) { VaultLogic.erc20IncreaseCrossSupply(debtAssetData, params.borrower, vars.remainDebtToLiquidate); } - creates supply shares from potential rounding artifacts

preconditions: Borrower has ERC721 cross collateral with debt in multiple groups
NFT collateral value exceeds total user debt (collateralTotalValue > sum of all group debts)
Rounding in rayMul/rayDiv creates non-zero remainDebtToLiquidate

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: remainDebtToLiquidate becomes supply shares; rounding in group-by-group repayment may not sum exactly to actualDebtToLiquidate
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: ERC721 cross liquidations with multi-group debt

operational_questions: What is the maximum rounding error across multiple group repayments?
Can the remainder be large enough to be economically significant?
Does the phantom supply eventually dilute other suppliers?
validation_requirements: Test multi-group ERC721 liquidation with edge-case scaled amounts
Measure remainDebtToLiquidate value in various scenarios
Verify supply/debt conservation after liquidation

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Create position with debt spread across many groups with specific scaled amounts that maximize rounding
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Liquidation creates phantom supply shares for the borrower from rounding
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Borrower withdraws the phantom supply shares as real tokens
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires specific position setup and cooperative liquidator

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Small accounting discrepancies per liquidation; cumulative dilution of supplier positions
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Minor per-event; significant if repeated at scale

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Advanced - ray math edge case exploitation

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Non-zero remainDebtToLiquidate events in ERC721 liquidations
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Repeated liquidations creating supply for borrowers

threats.title: Isolate Redeem Enables Debt Reduction for Wrong User Creating Permanent Phantom Debt and NFT Lock
threats.hypothesis_description: As a consequence of OPS-H003, after a third-party redeem corrupts accounting, the original borrower's userScaledIsolateBorrow remains inflated while their loan's scaledAmount was reduced. When the borrower later attempts full repayment of the loan, the loan's scaledAmount may be fully repaid, but their userScaledIsolateBorrow still shows excess. This phantom debt prevents the accountCheckAndSetBorrowedAsset from clearing the borrow flag, and the excess userScaledIsolateBorrow can never be decreased because there's no corresponding loan to repay against. The NFT may become permanently locked if the locker check relies on loan status.
threats.investigation_rationale: After corrupted redeem: loanData.scaledAmount is X, userScaledIsolateBorrow[borrower] is X + delta (where delta = the amount incorrectly decreased from the wrong user). When borrower repays the loan fully (paying X * borrowIndex), the loan is deleted and lockerAddr cleared. But userScaledIsolateBorrow[borrower] still has delta remaining. This phantom balance can never be cleared because there's no loan to repay. Any future HF calculations that include isolate borrows would see phantom debt.

proof_of_signal.pattern: accountCheckAndSetBorrowedAsset checks erc20GetUserScaledCrossBorrowInAsset (cross only, not isolate); but phantom isolate debt from redeem bug persists in groupData.userScaledIsolateBorrow

preconditions: OPS-H003 has already occurred (corrupted redeem)
Original borrower attempts full loan repayment
groupData.userScaledIsolateBorrow[borrower] > sum(loanData.scaledAmount for borrower's active loans)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: accountCheckAndSetBorrowedAsset only checks cross borrows, not isolate borrows, so the phantom debt might not trigger visible issues in cross-margin HF, but totalScaledIsolateBorrow becomes inconsistent

operational_questions: Does the phantom isolate debt affect any cross-margin health factor calculations?
Can the phantom debt cause underflow in future isolate operations?
Is there any admin mechanism to correct the phantom balance?
validation_requirements: Trace full lifecycle: borrow → auction → corrupted redeem → repay → verify phantom debt remains
Test if borrower can withdraw NFT after full repay despite phantom debt
Test if phantom debt causes issues in new isolate borrows

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Trigger OPS-H003 (third party redeems someone else's auctioned loan)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Original borrower fully repays their remaining loan
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Phantom userScaledIsolateBorrow remains for the borrower
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Depending on how isolate borrows interact with other protocol functions, this could cause cascading issues

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Permanent accounting inconsistency; potential cascading failures in future operations
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires protocol upgrade to clear phantom balances
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Affected user's future isolate lending operations

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Users with userScaledIsolateBorrow > 0 but no active isolate loans
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Failed repay or withdraw operations for affected users
